name: Guadalhorce Reservoirs Loop
name_original: Vuelta al Embalse de Guadalhorce – ruta circular
description: Experience challenging road cycling with fantastic views of Embalse de Guadalhorce and the Caminito del Rey in Andalusia, Spain.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Ardales traverse a landscape characterized by mountains, deep gorges, and a series of large reservoirs. The region features limestone cliffs, pine forests, and rocky terrain, offering varied scenery for cyclists. Elevation changes are common, with routes incorporating sections around features like the Sierra de la Huma. This area provides a diverse backdrop for road cycling, from moderate to challenging. Best road cycling routes around Ardales The most popular road cycling route is Guadalhorce Reservoirs Loop, a 19.7 miles (31.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 49 minutes to complete. This moderate route circles the scenic Guadalhorce Reservoirs. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is View of Caminito del Rey – View of Álora Castle loop from Ardales, a difficult 42.2 miles (68.0 km) path. This route offers views of the Caminito del Rey and extends towards Álora Castle. Local road cyclists also love the Caminito del Rey – Steep climb in El Chorro loop from Parking Ardales, a 23.4 miles (37.7 km) trail leading through the El Chorro area, often completed in about 2 hours 8 minutes. Road cycling around Ardales is defined by its reservoirs, the Gaitanes Gorge, and limestone cliffs. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to very challenging. The routes in Ardales are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 30 reviews. More than 1,600 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Ardales's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Ardales?
A: Ardales offers a diverse selection of nearly 20 road cycling routes. These routes cater to various fitness levels, with a good mix of moderate and challenging options to explore the region's unique landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Ardales?
A: Road cycling in Ardales features a captivating blend of mountains, deep gorges, and large reservoirs. You'll encounter limestone cliffs, lush pine forests, and rocky terrain. Elevation changes are common, with routes often incorporating sections around features like the Sierra de la Huma, providing varied and engaging scenery.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Ardales?
A: The region around Ardales is generally favorable for road cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures, making them ideal. Summers can be hot, so early morning or late afternoon rides are recommended. Winters are typically mild, though some higher elevation routes might be cooler.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Ardales?
A: While many routes in Ardales involve significant elevation, there are moderate options that can be suitable for cyclists with good fitness levels. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e932244191/guadalhorce-reservoirs-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalhorce Reservoirs Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 19.7-mile ride that circles the scenic reservoirs, offering a less strenuous experience compared to more mountainous routes.

Q: Where can I find challenging road cycling routes in Ardales?
A: Ardales is an excellent destination for experienced road cyclists seeking a challenge. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1275478" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Caminito del Rey – View of Álora Castle loop from Ardales&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 42.2-mile path with over 1,200 meters of elevation gain, offer substantial climbs and rewarding descents through dramatic landscapes.

Q: What scenic viewpoints or natural features will I encounter on Ardales road cycling routes?
A: You'll be treated to stunning natural beauty. Routes often circle the dazzling blue &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3782093" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Conde de Guadalhorce Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; and other Ardales Lakes. Many paths also offer breathtaking views of the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1308367" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gaitanes Gorge&lt;/a&gt; and the iconic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3834062" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Caminito del Rey&lt;/a&gt;. The Sierra de la Huma also provides panoramic vistas.

Q: Are there many circular road cycling routes around Ardales?
A: Yes, Ardales offers numerous circular road cycling routes, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Popular examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e932244191/guadalhorce-reservoirs-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalhorce Reservoirs Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40015360" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Caminito del Rey – Steep climb in El Chorro loop from Parking Ardales&lt;/a&gt;, which provide varied distances and difficulties.

Q: Can I find road cycling routes that pass through other towns near Ardales?
A: Absolutely. Many road cycling routes from Ardales extend to connect with charming nearby towns. You can find routes that lead towards locations such as El Chorro, Álora, and even longer expeditions towards Ronda or Antequera, offering diverse challenges and opportunities to explore the wider Andalusian countryside.

Q: Is parking available for road cyclists near the Ardales lakes?
A: Yes, parking is generally available, especially near popular starting points for routes around the Ardales lakes and the Caminito del Rey area. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40015360" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Caminito del Rey – Steep climb in El Chorro loop from Parking Ardales&lt;/a&gt; specifically mentions starting from a parking area, indicating convenience for cyclists arriving by car.

Q: Are there cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in Ardales pass through or near small villages and popular tourist spots where you can find cafes, restaurants, and shops for refreshments. The areas around the Ardales lakes and El Chorro, in particular, offer options for a break during your ride.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about the routes in Ardales?
A: The road cycling routes in Ardales are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 30 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the stunning natural beauty, particularly the views of the reservoirs and the dramatic Gaitanes Gorge, as well as the varied terrain that offers both challenging climbs and scenic descents.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling routes in Ardales?
A: While many routes in Ardales feature significant elevation, families with older children or those comfortable with moderate climbs can enjoy some of the less demanding circular routes around the reservoirs. It's best to check the difficulty and elevation profile of specific routes to ensure they match your family's fitness level.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or attractions to see while road cycling near Ardales?
A: Beyond the stunning natural landscapes, you can cycle past significant attractions. The famous &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3834062" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Caminito del Rey&lt;/a&gt; and its impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5422880" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Suspension Bridge over the Gaitanes Gorge&lt;/a&gt; are often visible from cycling paths. You might also encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4437799" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gaitanejo Reservoir Dam&lt;/a&gt;, which is an interesting man-made structure amidst the natural beauty.
tips: A picturesque site where taking a photo is definitely a must. Its turquoise waters make the landscape magical. Built in the years 1914 - 1921, it is one of the set of reservoirs of the middle course of the Guadalhorce river.

A magnificent viewpoint with a unique natural setting. The water of the reservoirs shimmers a truly turquoise color, offering incredible views.

There is a parking lot not far away.

Mirador de los Tres Embalses (Viewpoint of the Three Reservoirs): Located at the confluence of the Turón, Guadalteba, and Guadalhorce reservoirs, the viewpoint offers a panoramic view of the various dams and hydroelectric power plants of the El Chorro water network. This viewpoint was originally built as a topographical checkpoint during construction in the early 20th century.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is considered a challenging road bike ride, requiring very good fitness. It features significant elevation gain and some segments, while primarily paved, may be unpaved and difficult, potentially requiring you to push your bike. The region is known for its steeper climbs.

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 31.8 km (19.7 miles) long and is estimated to take about 4 hours to complete by racebike, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: Where does the route start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The route starts from El Chorro. You can find parking in the El Chorro area. Many visitors park near the train station or designated areas for the Caminito del Rey, which is close by.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: You'll primarily ride on quiet rural paved roads, but be aware that some segments may be unpaved and challenging. The landscape is characterized by limestone mountain ridges, sandstone hills, and dramatic gorges.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: This route offers consistently fantastic views of the Conde de Guadalhorce Reservoir and the stunning mountain landscapes. You'll also pass by the Three Reservoirs Viewpoint and get glimpses of the suspension bridge of the Caminito del Rey over the Rio Guadalhorce gorge.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other official trails. You'll find it intersects with parts of the Andalucía (Provincia de Málaga), GR 7 - Andalucía (Provincia de Málaga), Campillos - Embalses del Guadalhorce, and Embalses del Guadalhorce - Estación del Chorro trails.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As this is a road cycling route, it's generally not recommended for dogs to accompany cyclists, especially given the challenging terrain and potential for traffic on some sections. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are safely secured in a bike trailer designed for pets and check local regulations for the Desfiladero de los Gaitanes Natural Area.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region generally offers pleasant cycling conditions outside of the peak summer heat. Spring and autumn are ideal, with milder temperatures and beautiful scenery. Winter can also be good, though some days might be cooler or wetter.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are required to cycle this route within the Desfiladero de los Gaitanes Natural Area. However, if you plan to visit the nearby Caminito del Rey, that requires a separate ticket and reservation.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to refuel along the route?
faqs.answer: While the route itself traverses quiet rural roads, the starting point in El Chorro offers some amenities. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially given the route's challenging nature and the serene, less populated inland roads.

faqs.question: What's an insider tip for cycling this route?
faqs.answer: To fully appreciate the stunning views and geological formations, consider starting your ride early in the morning. This allows you to enjoy the serene inland roads with minimal traffic and experience the changing landscape with each turn in cooler conditions before the midday sun.
